question: 1. Mr. DeSoto was pleased with the responses from the students. How can he reinforce these and other reading strategies through think alouds and other approaches to ensure students are using them when they read? Base your response on what you know about best practices in literacy instruction.
answer: 1. Mr. DeSoto and the students could construct a Reading Strategies bulletin board on which they display the reading strategies they could use to help them comprehend texts. During group discussions of passages, they could share what strategies they used with particularly difficult parts. Also, students could keep a journal as they read. In the journal they could reflect on the meanings they are making from the readings and the reading comprehension strategies they are using successfully and unsuccessfully. Mr. DeSoto should do think alouds in which he is not successful using a particular strategy and show how he works through several strategies to help him finally arrive at an understanding of the text. Finally, he should invite students to demonstrate how they think aloud through strategies as they read.

## PLT Case History 6.1

Directions: Read the following case history, then answer the three short-answer questions that follow. You are not expected to include citations of specific texts, authors, or theories in your responses. Your answers will be evaluated, however, based on your use of professionally accepted practices and principles in learning and teaching. Some questions have multiple parts, so be sure your response addresses all components of each question.

## Mr. DeSoto

Mr. DeSoto had several students in his tenth-grade literature class that he suspected were having trouble with the class textbook. They could decode the text. That wasn't the problem. They seemed to have trouble understanding the messages behind the words. So, he decided to spend part of each class day trying a method a colleague told him she had begun to use successfully in her class: think alouds.

The first day Mr. DeSoto tried the think-aloud method, he read a paragraph from A Midsummer Night's Dream. As he read, he stopped after a particular sentence he thought might be confusing to students and said, "I don't know what this sentence means." Then, he restated the difficult sentence and said, "I still don't know what it means, so I think I'll look back through the passage and see if I can find information that might give me a clue to the meaning of the sentence I don't understand."

This is how Mr. DeSoto continued through reading and thinking aloud for about 5 minutes. When he was done, he asked his students what he had just done to help understand passages he didn't understand at first. Also, he asked his students what they did when they came to parts that confused them. These are some of the responses he got from his students:

"You talked your way through the parts you didn't understand. Sometimes when I'm

reading something and having trouble with it, I read it out loud. For some reason, that helps me figure things out sometimes."

"You looked for clues. You read parts over again. Rereading things can help."

"You stopped when you realized you didn't understand what you'd just read. I usually just keep going and hope that eventually I'll figure it out. I don't like to read things again and again. You don't have to understand every sentence to get an idea of what's happening. As long as you understand most of it, you're usually all right."

## PLT Case History 6.1 Questions

question: 2. Describe one authentic approach Mr. DeSoto can use to assess students' reading comprehension of A Midsummer Night's Dream as they are reading it. Explain two ways that the approach you chose is authentic.
answer: 2. An authentic approach Mr. DeSoto could use is to have the students write Readers Theater scripts of the passages they read and perform the scripts. Their group performances will show how they made meaning of their reading. Readers Theater is authentic in that it requires students to show how they have interpreted meaning from the text. It requires students to use higher-order thinking skills. Also, Readers Theater is a form of authentic assessment because it connects the readings to personal experiences to which the students can relate. The performance of the script for classmates provides a real-world audience that will respond authentically.

## PLT Case History 6.1

Directions: Read the following case history, then answer the three short-answer questions that follow. You are not expected to include citations of specific texts, authors, or theories in your responses. Your answers will be evaluated, however, based on your use of professionally accepted practices and principles in learning and teaching. Some questions have multiple parts, so be sure your response addresses all components of each question.

## Mr. DeSoto

Mr. DeSoto had several students in his tenth-grade literature class that he suspected were having trouble with the class textbook. They could decode the text. That wasn't the problem. They seemed to have trouble understanding the messages behind the words. So, he decided to spend part of each class day trying a method a colleague told him she had begun to use successfully in her class: think alouds.

The first day Mr. DeSoto tried the think-aloud method, he read a paragraph from A Midsummer Night's Dream. As he read, he stopped after a particular sentence he thought might be confusing to students and said, "I don't know what this sentence means." Then, he restated the difficult sentence and said, "I still don't know what it means, so I think I'll look back through the passage and see if I can find information that might give me a clue to the meaning of the sentence I don't understand."

This is how Mr. DeSoto continued through reading and thinking aloud for about 5 minutes. When he was done, he asked his students what he had just done to help understand passages he didn't understand at first. Also, he asked his students what they did when they came to parts that confused them. These are some of the responses he got from his students:

"You talked your way through the parts you didn't understand. Sometimes when I'm

reading something and having trouble with it, I read it out loud. For some reason, that helps me figure things out sometimes."

"You looked for clues. You read parts over again. Rereading things can help."

"You stopped when you realized you didn't understand what you'd just read. I usually just keep going and hope that eventually I'll figure it out. I don't like to read things again and again. You don't have to understand every sentence to get an idea of what's happening. As long as you understand most of it, you're usually all right."

## PLT Case History 6.1 Questions

question: 3. Besides think alouds, describe one other method Mr. DeSoto could use to teach students reading comprehension strategies like the ones he taught during the think aloud example.
answer:
3. A method Mr. DeSoto could use to teach students reading comprehension strategies is to use explicit strategy instruction. After observing and surveying students to determine what strategies would help them, bring together a small group of students who would benefit from a mini-lesson on a particular strategy. Collect several passages from classroom readings. Select passages that will help you model the strategy. Make them into slides in a PowerPoint presentation or use whatever form of technology you have available to display the passages. First, activate the students' prior knowledge about the passage's topic. Then, introduce the strategy you will be teaching them, describe it through an example, explain why it is beneficial, and discuss when it could be used. Model using it with a couple of passages, then provide guided and independent practice with other passages until the students seem confident with its use.

## PLT Case History 6.2 Keanna Petersen

1. To address Keanna's needs, her teacher could try these strategies. He could present ideas in different ways. He could, for example, present new information through a graphic organizer, explain the information orally, provide readings, and then talk with Keanna to determine what gaps of understanding still exist. He could also extend conversations and discussions about topics, considering oral language is the foundation for written language. Practicing and hearing new vocabulary and names before being expected to read about them will help Keanna when she sees them in writing. Using the new terms and names in small-group discussions or think-pair-share groups will help her self-monitor her understandings, too.

2. There are a number of factors that influence adolescents' decline in motivation. At their age, many begin to question their goals, values, and belief systems. They may question a grading system that is used to place students and determine if they merit retention, promotion, and even graduation. They question expectations that compare them to how well they do in comparison with their peers. Many adolescents begin to compare themselves to their peers and start to lose confidence in their abilities. They begin to see school not as learning and mastering subjects but as performing well in comparison to their peers. As a result, motivation declines.

3. Mr. Elkin can influence and support his students' motivation. He can set clear expectations and goals for all of the assignments. If he expects all students to come to class prepared, he should find out why Keanna is not completing the work. Perhaps she is having difficulty and would benefit from coming in for extra help or being assigned a homework tutor. Recognizing and providing support while maintaining high expectations will influence Keanna's motivation to meet the teacher's expectations. Mr. Elkin should also help Keanna set personal goals for her progress while she is in Schoharie. She is not motivated by the grades but may be motivated by goals she sets for herself. When provided support to achieve personal goals through the guidance of Mr. Elkin, Keanna will be self-motivated to meet these goals.

## PLT Case History 6.3 Mr. Granato

1. There are advantages and disadvantages to parents being highly involved in their children's academic lives. One advantage is the belief that any attention is better than no attention. "Helicopter parents" watch over their children and advocate for them. They model good behaviors and ways to get things accomplished. In this case, Mrs. Maynard is modeling organization and follow-through, and how to talk with teachers and others in positions of authority. The disadvantages would include the issue that helicopter parents often seem to overstep their bounds by talking for the student when he or she should be advocating for him- or herself. There comes a point where Cody has to become responsible for himself. In life there probably won't be someone who is always supporting, pushing, and looking out for him. He has to learn to do this himself.

2. By the definition of a “helicopter parent” given in question 1, Mrs. Maynard can be identified as one. She has micromanaged Cody’s academic life over the past 13 years by constantly contacting his teachers, checking his homework, and checking up on his progress. Although she is doing her job as a parent, she seems to be overbearing. One could presume she has caused Cody to be semi-interested in school. He can actually sit back and let his mother do things for him that he should be doing. For example, if he has a question about homework, he should be the one asking the teacher, not his mother. To be motivated, students should feel they are in positions of power, authority, and ownership to be engaged in meaningful learning.

3. So far, the meeting is not going as well as one might hope. Mrs. Maynard seems to be in charge and ready to spring at Mr. Granato, putting him on defense. The purpose of the meeting is to share ideas and get to know each other, though. Hopefully, Mr. Granato can have a chance before Mrs. Maynard has to leave, to talk with both parties to come up with a plan to improve Cody's motivation and enthusiasm about the academic focus of twelfth-grade. It would be helpful if he could help Cody articulate what would motivate him to be more engaged in the class and to be in command of his own learning. He should treat Mrs. Maynard respectfully and give her credit for trying to support Cody.

## PLT Case History 6.4 Ms. Strub

1. Ms. Strub should be concerned about Annie's self-concept, feelings of isolation, and how they will influence her confidence and academics. Ms. Strub should continue to observe and write anecdotal notes in her journal to document specific behaviors, comments, and interactions that seem positive or troublesome. (Note the positive should be documented also so Ms. Strub can analyze contexts that cause positive responses and can capitalize on them.) Adolescence can be a troublesome time. Perhaps Annie is just going through a rough stage. Ms. Strub should check with past teachers and when she has enough evidence to support any concerns she has, she should talk with Annie's family to gain further insight. Ms. Strub's main concerns should be Annie's mental health and how Ms. Strub can best meet Annie's learning needs as a result.

2. We don't know enough about Annie and about how Ms. Strub runs her classes at this point to predict how Annie's social and emotional status might influence her learning in Ms. Strub's class. If Ms. Strub values collaboration, cooperation, and group work in her class, Annie could react in two ways: She could recoil and not participate due to her shyness, or she could become more comfortable working in small groups intimately with people. Annie seems to crave authentic friendship, which may develop as a result of getting to know people in safe contexts, such as small groups. Ms. Strub will have to keep a close eye on how Annie reacts to various methods so she can design lessons that will help Annie develop socially, emotionally, and academically.

3. During adolescence, young women like Annie are susceptible to developing drug or alcohol dependence. Adolescence is depicted as a time young people demonstrate risky behaviors and recklessness. They are trying out their newfound independence from their families and trying to shape identities for themselves. They try new things, sometimes to be accepted as part of a group of peers. Acceptance into peer groups is extremely important to adolescents as shown in Annie's poem. Furthermore, although most young people are psychologically healthy, they can begin to exhibit signs of mental illness. Mood disorders (i.e., clinical depression, bipolar disorder, and anxiety disorders) may emerge during late adolescence. Suicide is a leading cause of death in adolescence, so it is an educator's responsibility to watch for signs of teens who are dealing with what they consider to be severe emotional trauma (i.e., struggling with peer relationships, being bullied, pressure from parents, and feelings of not belonging). Adolescence can be a time of turmoil and self-doubt. It appears Annie is struggling as evidenced in her poem and behaviors.

## Multiple-Choice Questions I

1. a. With reciprocal teaching, students learn to use these four interrelated strategies when reading across the content areas: question, clarify, summarize, and predict.  
2. b. GIST is a summarization strategy students use. In the GIST approach, students write a 15-word summary of an article, then work to delete trivial propositions to choose the main idea. Then they select topic statements that fit the 15-word summary.  
3. a. An anticipation/reaction guide assesses students' knowledge before they begin a unit of study or a lesson and again after they participate in the unit or lesson. The guide consists of a list of statements the student marks as true or false. They indicate what the students know or anticipate being true or false and then what they learned about the topic after study.

4. The table is an example of

a. a rubric.  
b. a portfolio.  
c. an interest chart.  
d. an inquiry chart.
answer: 4. d. An inquiry chart is used to look at critical questions by integrating what the student already knows about a topic and what information is gained from various resources.

## PLT Case History 6.4 Directions: Read the following case history, then answer the three short-answer questions that follow. You are not expected to include citations of specific texts, authors, or theories in your responses. Your answers will be evaluated, however, based on your use of professionally accepted practices and principles in learning and teaching. Some questions have multiple parts, so be sure your response addresses all components of each question. ## Ms. Strub Dusti Strub is a first-year seventh-grade English teacher at Onteora Middle School. She has made it through the middle of her first month of teaching. Things have gone pretty smoothly, and she feels she is getting to know the students very well. She spent time getting to know her students first. She had them write “I Am” poems—a formulaic poem about how authors see themselves—to introduce themselves. This activity also gave her an idea of their writing skills and their interests so she could use what she’d learned to plan her next writing assignment. Ms. Strub connects well with this age group, she is finding. However, there is one student who Ms. Strub is particularly concerned about. Annie Morris is her name. She's a very quiet young girl with short blonde hair and big brown eyes. She rarely looks Ms. Strub in the eyes even when Ms. Strub has stopped her in the hall just to converse. Ms. Strub has noticed Annie seems to tag along with two girls who have very different personalities from her own. Ms. Strub would describe Annie's place in the threesome as more like an awkward third wheel than part of a close circle of friends. Ms. Strub has begun an observation journal about the students, and she is focusing on Annie at the moment. She is mostly concerned based on the I Am poem Annie wrote, but the young woman's behaviors and self-imposed isolation also have her concerned. ## Annie's I Am Poem I am ugly and shy. I wonder if there is life after death. I hear people talking about me. I see snickers and sneers. I want to have lots of friends. I am ugly and shy. I pretend I am popular. I feel like an alien on a strange planet. I touch the stars and the far planets. I worry about what other people say about me. I cry when I am alone. I am ugly and shy. I understand that being popular isn't important, it's what's inside that has value. I say, "I can get through this." I dream about living in Australia. I try to smile at people. I hope they notice me. I am ugly and shy. ## PLT Case History 6.4 Questions 1. What concerns should Ms. Strub have about Annie? What should Ms. Strub do about her concerns? Base your responses on what you know about dealing with adolescents' social and emotional issues. 2. How might Annie's social and emotional status influence her learning in Ms. Strub's class? 3. If unchecked or ignored, adolescent's social and emotional issues can lead to mental health issues. Discuss TWO psychological issues adolescents are susceptible to develop during this stage of development. ## Multiple-Choice Questions I 1. Students learn to question, clarify, summarize, and predict to improve their ability to monitor their understanding of text as they apply strategies on their own through a. reciprocal teaching. b. guided teaching. c. extended text teaching. d. scaffolded teaching. 2. According to the Generating Interaction between Schemata and Text (GIST) approach, students a. add a main idea with supporting ideas to a writing prompt. b. construct a 15-word-limit summary of a text with topic statements that fit it. c. practice extending their writing by adding personal experiences to it. d. activate prior knowledge to interpret text. 3. An anticipation/reaction guide for the topic of dinosaurs might include which of the following? a. Most dinosaurs' names come from the Latin language. b. Explain how you feel about dinosaurs. c. What do you want to learn about dinosaurs? d. What do you already know about dinosaurs? Use the following table to answer question 4. QUESTION 1 QUESTION 2 QUESTION 3 QUESTION 4 QUESTION 5

question:
6. The findings of the National Institute for Literacy indicate

a. content-area teachers are not responsible for improving adolescent literacy skills.  
b. the primary responsibility of content-area teachers should be to improve adolescents' literacy skills.  
c. content-area teachers must be aware of literacy instructional approaches that can be integrated within their existing curricula.  
d. the needs of diverse learners cannot be assessed accurately by content-area teachers.
answer: 6. c. Content-area teachers, in order to be effective, must know how to improve the literacy levels of struggling readers and writers whom they work with in their classes.

question:
7. Which of the following is an example of self-regulation?

a. A student working in a collaborative group of peers is in charge of assessing that all members of the group are doing their assigned jobs.  
b. A reader is motivated, has prior knowledge about a topic, and uses effective strategies as she reads a passage.  
c. A writer directs his performance toward goals he wants to achieve.  
d. A writer perceives that she has control over her writing.
answer: 7. b. A self-regulated reader or writer understands the tasks of an activity and uses effective strategies that help him or her complete the tasks. Self-regulation refers to understanding the task so the reader or writer knows how to perform to achieve his or her goals.

I feel like an alien on a strange planet. I touch the stars and the far planets. I worry about what other people say about me. I cry when I am alone. I am ugly and shy. I understand that being popular isn't important, it's what's inside that has value. I say, "I can get through this." I dream about living in Australia. I try to smile at people. I hope they notice me. I am ugly and shy. ## PLT Case History 6.4 Questions 1. What concerns should Ms. Strub have about Annie? What should Ms. Strub do about her concerns? Base your responses on what you know about dealing with adolescents' social and emotional issues. 2. How might Annie's social and emotional status influence her learning in Ms. Strub's class? 3. 8. The most common form of formative assessment at the middle- and high-school levels is

a. state tests across the content areas.  
b. teacher questioning.  
c. performance criteria.  
d. the use of student think alouds.
answer: 8. b. Teacher questioning is the most common form of formative assessment. Questioning may occur orally or in writing. However, teachers should not rely solely on questioning techniques.

She has made it through the middle of her first month of teaching. Things have gone pretty smoothly, and she feels she is getting to know the students very well. She spent time getting to know her students first. She had them write “I Am” poems—a formulaic poem about how authors see themselves—to introduce themselves. This activity also gave her an idea of their writing skills and their interests so she could use what she’d learned to plan her next writing assignment. Ms. Strub connects well with this age group, she is finding. However, there is one student who Ms. Strub is particularly concerned about. Annie Morris is her name. She's a very quiet young girl with short blonde hair and big brown eyes. She rarely looks Ms. Strub in the eyes even when Ms. Strub has stopped her in the hall just to converse. Ms. Strub has noticed Annie seems to tag along with two girls who have very different personalities from her own. Ms. Strub would describe Annie's place in the threesome as more like an awkward third wheel than part of a close circle of friends. Ms. Strub has begun an observation journal about the students, and she is focusing on Annie at the moment. She is mostly concerned based on the I Am poem Annie wrote, but the young woman's behaviors and self-imposed isolation also have her concerned. ## Annie's I Am Poem I am ugly and shy. question:
9. Morphology refers to

a. the part of speech a word represents.  
b. the ability to use context clues to decipher the meaning of a word.  
c. the definition of a word.  
d. the roots and affixes in a word.
answer: 9. d. Morphology refers to the linguistic ability to identify and describe the components of a word, such as its root and affixes (prefixes and suffixes).

question:
10. G. Stanley Hall was one of the first psychologists to study adolescents. He is noted for identifying the period between ages 12 and 25 as a period of

a. childhood and adulthood.  
b. storm and stress.  
c. laughter and frivolity.  
d. fragility and strength.
answer: 10. b. The psychologist G. Stanley Hall referred to the years of adolescence as those of storm and stress, reflecting the turbulence experienced due to the adolescent's transitioning from childhood to adulthood.

question:
11. According to the National Center for Education Statistics, the dropout rates of high school students between 2000 and 2008

a. differed by race and ethnicity.  
b. increased drastically due to unemployment.  
c. increased drastically due to changes in housing.  
d. remained the same.
answer: 11. a. Between 2000 and 2008, the dropout rates of high school students differed by race and ethnicity. While overall, the rates decreased; the rates were lower for Whites and Blacks than for Hispanics and Asians/Pacific Islanders.

question:
12. If a parent or a school district has concerns about the eligibility or placement of a child in a supportive special education program, either party has the legal right to

a. request a due process hearing to resolve the dispute.  
b. enforce a solution they deem appropriate for the child.  
c. void the child's individualized education plan.  
d. place the child in the program based on assignment as the child's power of attorney.

## Multiple-Choice Questions II

1. Word identification or decoding refers to

a. the ability to figure out the word a group of accurately sequenced letters makes.  
b. knowing the definition of a word in isolation.  
c. determining the meaning of a word by using context clues.  
d. the ability to pick the word a person dictates out of a list of words.

2. According to the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA), children with disabilities require Individualized Education Programs (IEPs). The following information about the child must be included; however, this is not the only information that is required by law to be documented in the IEP. The IEP must include

a. past performance, short- and long-term goals for the child, and health history of the child  
b. past performance, current skills, goals, special education services  
c. current performance, annual goals, and special education and related services  
d. needed transitional services, family history, and how progress will be measured

3. Erikson's eight stages of development propose that adolescents (ages 12 to 18 years)

a. are developing a sense of industry accompanied by unresolved feelings of inferiority among their peers.  
b. attempt to find their own identity and experience role confusion as they separate from family to be members of a wider society.  
c. experience a desire to take initiative while they rely significantly on the basic family structure.  
d. experience intimacy or isolation, depending on their success with this stage.

4. The main objective for PL 94-142 is to

a. mainstream and integrate students with handicaps into general education classes.  
b. provide equal education for children of all cultures and races.  
c. extend health insurance for all students living with their parents up to the age of 25.  
d. restrict the working age for children to 16.

5. If a parent wanted to know what the goals of the science program of a high school were and what fundamental concepts would be explored, he or she would ask the administrator for a copy of

a. the school's individualized education program.  
b. the school's mission statement.  
c. the school's report card.  
d. the school's curriculum guide.

6. At the beginning of each class, it is a good idea for educators to review with the students what happened in the previous class

a. to give students time to settle down and get ready for the day's lesson.  
b. to reinforce students' understanding of where they are now and to determine if any reteaching is needed.  
c. so students can summarize what they are to do in this class.  
d. because research indicates this is the best way to begin a class using the constructivist approach.

7. Which of the following is the best example of a question/prompt that would facilitate a student's factual recall?

a. How does the character John Proctor in The Crucible compare with other characters you know?  
b. How did you get your answer to problem four?  
c. Describe the contexts in which nominalization would be used.  
d. Is the solution a reasonable one considering the situation?

8. Families are sometimes marginalized by cultural or linguistic differences and need the teacher's help to know effective ways to support school goals. When working with families that are marginalized, the teacher and school

a. must not judge these families for the lack of interest they portray by not attending school events.  
b. should hold families accountable to attend school events.  
c. must find ways to accommodate families who are not able to attend school events.  
d. should not plan school events for these families because they will not be able to participate and will feel out of place.

9. Constructivist approaches are most effective

a. when instructors are expert at teaching students how to construct answers, not at giving them answers.  
b. with unmotivated students.  
c. when teachers eliminate direct instruction from the curriculum.  
d. when students are not expected to memorize.

10. Nonfluent speakers typically will have difficulty articulating the English language when reading a text out loud. Therefore, the teacher should

a. not call on these students to read text out loud in class so as not to embarrass them.  
b. recognize the students cannot understand a passage if they cannot accurately pronounce the words out loud.  
c. understand nonfluent speakers of the English language can comprehend what they are reading before they can correctly pronounce all of the sounds in the language.  
d. assume the reading skills of the nonfluent speaker are well established and it is just an articulation problem.

Questions 11 and 12 can be answered by reading the following passages. They are taken from a debate about the advantages and disadvantages of discussing controversial issues in the secondary classroom with students.

## Controversial Issues Should Be Discussed with High-School Students

Teachers must accept the incredible responsibility of helping secondary students understand and make sense of today's social and political events. Today's youth are intelligent and world-savvy. They have access to media and news from around the world at the touch of a screen. Therefore, we cannot assume they are disinterested in world, political, and social events. Neither should we assume they can intelligently interpret all they hear without the guidance of those adults in their lives who have more education and experiences than the students have. High-school students are generally eager to discuss controversial issues. Frequently, it is the students who bring these topics up when they are inserted into classroom discussions. We owe it to our students to offer our classrooms as safe grounds for questioning and clarifying controversial issues so students can make informed decisions and construct meaningful positions on them. Many times controversy is inherent in content areas; dismissing it would be irresponsible. Educators are supposed to help students develop skills needed to interpret today's world. It is the duty of educators to carefully plan contexts so students can explore issues in a constructive manner.

## High-School Classrooms Are Not the Place to Discuss Controversial Issues

Controversial issues are too risky and too unpredictable to bring into classroom conversations. Teachers are not equipped to know how to respond to current controversial issues. Even experts need time to understand causes and likely political and social effects of current issues to develop the knowledge and evidence needed to wisely counteract hearsay and speculation. Classroom discussions can cause students to compound their anxieties and fears. When they hear the speculations and fears of their classmates, they become doubtful and afraid. It is not the teacher's responsibility to bring controversial issues into the high-school classroom. It is the role of the family to address these concerns.

11. The author of the first article

a. would support teachers telling their students about the World Trade Center attacks on September 11, 2001, but not discussing it beyond that.  
b. would not support teachers saying anything about the World Trade Center attacks.  
c. would support teachers opening the classroom to discuss the possible reasons for the attacks the day they happened.  
d. would advise teachers to carefully plan how they would lead a class discussion about the World Trade Center attacks.

12. The most important component missing from both articles is

a. a timeline for discussing controversial issues so teachers can implement it into their curriculum.  
b. a definition of the term controversial issue because readers don't even know to what types of issues the authors are referring.  
c. the authors' names so we know what their political backgrounds are.  
d. the date the articles were written so we know if they are current or dated.
answer:
12. a. Due process hearings may be requested by either the parent or school when there is a dispute regarding the eligibility, placement, supports, or services necessary to ensure a free and appropriate public education that meets the student's needs. Due process is one of the six principles of the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA) of 2004.

## Multiple-Choice Questions II

1. a. Word identification or decoding is the ability to read a word or sound it out.  
2. c. An IEP must include current performance, annual goals, special education and related services, participation with nondisabled children, participation in state and district-wide tests, dates and places for services, transition service needs, needed transition services, age of majority, and statement of how progress will be measured.

3. b. Erikson's stage for adolescents refers to identity versus role confusion, in which development depends on what they do versus what others do for or to them.  
4. a. The main objective of PL 94-142 is to mainstream and integrate students with handicaps into general education classes as appropriate  
5. d. The curriculum guide is based on the state or local learning standards and typically describes the goals of a program as well as the topics that will be taught over a span of time, such as an academic year. Curriculum guides vary in the degree of specificity and content, but basically they articulate what will be taught.  
6. b. Reviewing material for the previous class with the students allows the educator to determine what the child remembers and understands from the previous class. If misunderstandings or gaps are revealed, the educator can reteach as needed. There is no evidence to support the other three choices.  
7. c. Choice c is the only one that requires only the recall of factual information. Other choices require the use of analysis and critical thinking skills. Factual recall questions/prompts typically start with words such as define, describe, explain, identify, locate, who, when, where, and what.  
8. c. Families are important connections to make. Schools should always attempt to accommodate families' schedules, transportation issues, and feelings of isolation.

9. a. Constructivist approaches require the "guide on the side" to be knowledgeable and skilled in facilitating students' learning to make sense of new information using what they already know. Constructivism does not require direct instruction or memorization be eliminated from instruction.  
10. c. Typically, nonfluent speakers can understand written English text before they can accurately pronounce all of the words out loud. The teacher should not make assumptions about the reading abilities of any student; rather, the teacher should review the permanent records of the student and follow up daily with formal and informal assessments of reading skills. Readers benefit from good models, and nonfluent speakers benefit from hearing others model accurate pronunciations.  
11. d. The first passage does not imply teachers should open their classrooms to any discussions. Choice d is the best choice because it refers to careful planning of the class discussion. The teacher would want to avoid scaring students with speculative hearsay, especially the day it occurred.  
12. b. The most important thing to know is how the authors are defining the term controversial issues. Readers don't know what the authors consider controversial. They could be very conservative and think teaching biology is controversial. Or, they could be very progressive and think that sex-change operation methods are appropriate to discuss with adolescents in a classroom situation.

## Praxis II Scoring

Evaluate how you did on this practice exam by scoring your short-answer responses and your multiple-choice answers. Bring your short-answer responses to a trusted friend to score so you can better see how you did. Reflect on the types of questions you missed most frequently.

The key to doing well on the Praxis II is to practice, read widely, and stay organized and focused. You have self-assessed your strengths and weaknesses and learned how to manage the various types of questions on the test. Enter the exam with confidence, knowing you are ready and equipped to do your very best!

She is mostly concerned based on the I Am poem Annie wrote, but the young woman's behaviors and self-imposed isolation also have her concerned. ## Annie's I Am Poem I am ugly and shy. I wonder if there is life after death. I hear people talking about me. I see snickers and sneers. I want to have lots of friends. I am ugly and shy. I pretend I am popular. I feel like an alien on a strange planet. I touch the stars and the far planets. I worry about what other people say about me. I cry when I am alone. I am ugly and shy. I understand that being popular isn't important, it's what's inside that has value. I say, "I can get through this." I dream about living in Australia. I try to smile at people. I hope they notice me. I am ugly and shy. ## PLT Case History 6.4 Questions 1. What concerns should Ms. Strub have about Annie? What should Ms. Strub do about her concerns? Base your responses on what you know about dealing with adolescents' social and emotional issues. 2. How might Annie's social and emotional status influence her learning in Ms. Strub's class? 3. If unchecked or ignored, adolescent's social and emotional issues can lead to mental health issues. Discuss TWO psychological issues adolescents are susceptible to develop during this stage of development. ## Multiple-Choice Questions I 1. Students learn to question, clarify, summarize, and predict to improve their ability to monitor their understanding of text as they apply strategies on their own through a. reciprocal teaching. b. guided teaching. c. extended text teaching. d. scaffolded teaching. 2.
